<!doctype html>
<html lang="en">
<head>
<meta charset="utf-8" />
<meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width,initial-scale=1" />
<title>Matroid theory — independence, duality, and the greedy algorithm</title>
<link rel="stylesheet" href="https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/katex@0.16.11/dist/katex.min.css">
<script defer src="https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/katex@0.16.11/dist/katex.min.js"></script>
<script defer src="https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/katex@0.16.11/dist/contrib/auto-render.min.js"></script>
<script>
(function(){
  function renderAll(){
    if(typeof renderMathInElement!=='function'){ return setTimeout(renderAll,50); }
    renderMathInElement(document.body,{
      delimiters:[
        {left:'$$',right:'$$',display:true},
        {left:'$',right:'$',display:false},
        {left:'\\(',right:'\\)',display:false},
        {left:'\\[',right:'\\]',display:true}
      ],
      throwOnError:false,
      macros:{
        '\\Spec':'\\Spec',
        '\\Gal':'\\Gal',
        '\\Hom':'\\Hom',
        '\\tr':'\\tr',
        '\\ad':'\\ad',
        '\\ind':'\\ind'
      }
    });
  }
  if(document.readyState==='loading') document.addEventListener('DOMContentLoaded',renderAll);
  else renderAll();
})();
</script>
<style>
  :root{
    --bg:#0f1218; --panel:#161b24; --panel2:#1c2230;
    --ink:#e8ecf1; --mute:#9aa4b2; --line:#2a3242;
    --yellow:#ffd866; --blue:#58c4dd; --green:#83c167;
    --pink:#e07a5f; --violet:#b39ddb; --cyan:#7de0d6;
  }
  *{box-sizing:border-box}
  html,body{margin:0;padding:0;background:var(--bg);color:var(--ink);
    font-family:-apple-system,BlinkMacSystemFont,"Segoe UI",Inter,Roboto,sans-serif;
    font-size:17px;line-height:1.65}
  a{color:var(--blue)}
  code{font-family:ui-monospace,SFMono-Regular,Menlo,Consolas,monospace;
    background:#0b0f16;padding:1px 6px;border-radius:4px;color:#f0c987;font-size:0.92em}
  main{max-width:880px;margin:0 auto;padding:4rem 1.5rem 8rem}
  h1{font-size:2.4rem;letter-spacing:-0.01em;margin:0 0 0.4rem}
  h2{font-size:1.6rem;margin:3.2rem 0 0.8rem;color:#fff;border-bottom:1px solid var(--line);padding-bottom:.4rem}
  h3{font-size:1.15rem;margin:1.8rem 0 .6rem;color:var(--yellow)}
  p{margin:0.7rem 0}
  .sub{color:var(--mute);margin-top:0;margin-bottom:2rem}
  .widget{background:var(--panel);border:1px solid var(--line);border-radius:10px;
    padding:1rem 1.1rem 1.2rem;margin:1.2rem 0}
  .widget .hd{display:flex;justify-content:space-between;align-items:baseline;margin-bottom:.6rem;gap:.8rem;flex-wrap:wrap}
  .widget .hd .ttl{font-weight:600;color:#fff;font-size:.95rem;letter-spacing:.02em;text-transform:uppercase}
  .widget .hd .hint{color:var(--mute);font-size:.85rem}
  .row{display:flex;gap:.6rem;flex-wrap:wrap;align-items:center;margin:.4rem 0}
  .row label{color:var(--mute);font-size:.88rem}
  input[type="text"],input[type="number"],select{
    background:#0b0f16;color:var(--ink);border:1px solid var(--line);
    border-radius:6px;padding:.35rem .55rem;font-family:inherit;font-size:.95rem}
  input[type="range"]{accent-color:var(--yellow);flex:1;max-width:260px}
  button{background:var(--panel2);color:var(--ink);border:1px solid var(--line);
    border-radius:6px;padding:.35rem .7rem;cursor:pointer;font:inherit}
  button:hover{background:#242c3e}
  button.active{background:var(--yellow);color:#111;border-color:var(--yellow)}
  .readout{background:#0b0f16;border:1px solid var(--line);border-radius:6px;
    padding:.6rem .8rem;font-family:ui-monospace,monospace;font-size:.92rem;color:#cbd5e1;
    min-height:1.6em;white-space:pre-wrap;word-break:break-word}
  .pill{display:inline-block;background:var(--panel2);border:1px solid var(--line);
    border-radius:999px;padding:1px 8px;font-size:.82rem;color:var(--mute);margin-right:4px}
  .note{border-left:3px solid var(--violet);padding:.3rem .9rem;color:#d8d2ef;background:#1a1828;
    margin:1rem 0;border-radius:0 6px 6px 0}
  .ok{border-left:3px solid var(--green);padding:.3rem .9rem;color:#d5ebc8;background:#1a2418;
    margin:1rem 0;border-radius:0 6px 6px 0}
  .bad{border-left:3px solid var(--pink);padding:.3rem .9rem;color:#f4d2c7;background:#2a1a18;
    margin:1rem 0;border-radius:0 6px 6px 0}
  svg{display:block;margin:0 auto;max-width:100%}
  svg text{fill:var(--ink);font-family:inherit}
  nav.toc{position:sticky;top:0;background:rgba(15,18,24,.93);backdrop-filter:blur(8px);
    border-bottom:1px solid var(--line);padding:.6rem 1rem;z-index:10;
    display:flex;gap:1rem;flex-wrap:wrap;font-size:.9rem}
  nav.toc a{color:var(--mute);text-decoration:none}
  nav.toc a:hover{color:var(--yellow)}
  /* sticky sidebar TOC (wide screens only; narrow screens fall back to top-nav) */
  aside.sidetoc{position:fixed;top:4.2rem;
    left:max(1rem, calc(50% - 440px - 220px));
    width:200px;max-height:calc(100vh - 6rem);overflow-y:auto;
    padding-right:1rem;border-right:1px solid var(--line);
    font-size:.88rem;z-index:5}
  aside.sidetoc .sidetoc-title{font-size:.7rem;letter-spacing:.14em;text-transform:uppercase;
    color:var(--mute);margin:0 0 .5rem;padding-bottom:.35rem;border-bottom:1px solid var(--line)}
  aside.sidetoc ol{list-style:none;padding:0;margin:0}
  aside.sidetoc li{margin:0}
  aside.sidetoc a{display:block;padding:.3rem .5rem;color:var(--mute);text-decoration:none;
    border-left:2px solid transparent;line-height:1.35;
    transition:color 120ms ease, border-color 120ms ease}
  aside.sidetoc a:hover{color:var(--ink)}
  aside.sidetoc a.active{color:var(--yellow);border-left-color:var(--yellow)}
  @media (max-width:1180px){ aside.sidetoc{display:none} }
  @media (min-width:1181px){ nav.toc a[href^="#"]{display:none} }
  .hero{padding:3rem 0 1rem}
  .small{font-size:.88rem;color:var(--mute)}
  footer{color:var(--mute);font-size:.88rem;text-align:center;margin-top:4rem}
  table.plain{width:100%;border-collapse:collapse;font-size:.95rem;margin:.6rem 0}
  table.plain th,table.plain td{padding:.35rem .5rem;border-bottom:1px solid var(--line);text-align:left}
  table.plain th{color:var(--mute);font-weight:500}
  /* callback-css-auto-begin */
  aside.callback{
    margin:1.2rem 0;padding:.7rem 1rem;
    background:rgba(88,196,221,0.05);
    border-left:3px solid color-mix(in srgb, var(--cyan) 45%, transparent);
    border-radius:0 6px 6px 0;
    font-size:.93rem;
  }
  aside.callback .ttl{
    font-size:.68rem;letter-spacing:.14em;text-transform:uppercase;
    color:var(--mute,#8c9aa6);margin-bottom:.3rem;font-weight:600;
  }
  aside.callback ul{margin:0;padding-left:1.1rem}
  aside.callback ul li{margin:.15rem 0}
  aside.callback a{color:inherit}
/* callback-css-auto-end */
  details.changelog{
    margin:2rem 0 1rem;padding:.6rem .9rem;
    background:rgba(0,0,0,0.22);
    border-radius:6px;
    font-size:.85rem;
  }
  details.changelog summary{
    cursor:pointer;letter-spacing:.1em;text-transform:uppercase;
    font-size:.7rem;color:var(--mute,#8c9aa6);
  }
  details.changelog table{margin-top:.5rem;border-collapse:collapse;width:100%;font-size:.82rem}
  details.changelog td{padding:.2rem .6rem;border-bottom:1px dashed rgba(255,255,255,0.07);vertical-align:top}
  details.changelog td:first-child{color:var(--mute,#8c9aa6);white-space:nowrap;font-variant-numeric:tabular-nums}
  aside.related{
    margin:1.2rem 0;padding:.7rem 1rem;
    background:color-mix(in srgb, var(--mute) 6%, transparent);
    border-left:3px solid color-mix(in srgb, var(--mute) 55%, transparent);
    border-radius:0 6px 6px 0;
    font-size:.93rem;
  }
  aside.related .ttl{
    font-size:.68rem;letter-spacing:.14em;text-transform:uppercase;
    color:var(--mute,#8c9aa6);margin-bottom:.3rem;font-weight:600;
  }
  aside.related > div{margin:.15rem 0}
  aside.related .more{color:var(--mute);font-style:italic}
  aside.related a{color:inherit}
  /* display-prefs-css-auto-begin */
  html[data-hide-widgets] .widget { display: none !important; }
  html[data-hide-quizzes] .quiz { display: none !important; }
  html[data-hide-lineage] .lineage-strip { display: none !important; }
  .mv-display-toggles { display: inline-flex; gap: .6rem; align-items: center; }
  .mv-display-toggle { background: transparent; border: 1px solid var(--line, #333); border-radius: 6px; padding: 5px 10px; cursor: pointer; font-size: 1.05rem; line-height: 1; color: inherit; transition: background 120ms ease, border-color 120ms ease; }
  .mv-display-toggle:hover { background: var(--panel, rgba(255,255,255,0.06)); border-color: var(--mute, #666); }
  .mv-display-toggle--off { opacity: 0.35; }
  /* display-prefs-css-auto-end */
</style>

Matroid theory

A matroid is the combinatorial skeleton shared by linear independence and graph acyclicity. The data is sparse — a finite ground set $E$ together with a family of "independent" subsets — yet it is enough to make the greedy algorithm provably optimal, to define a duality that swaps every theorem with its partner, and to encode a polynomial whose specializations include the chromatic polynomial, network reliability, and the partition function of a Potts model. This page traces the cryptomorphic axiom systems and their interplay.

blocks.html:
1. Independence axioms

Whitney's 1935 starting point. Fix a finite ground set $E$. A matroid on $E$ is a non-empty family $\mathcal{I} \subseteq 2^E$ of subsets called independent sets, satisfying:

  • (I1) Non-triviality. $\emptyset \in \mathcal{I}$.
  • (I2) Hereditary. If $I \in \mathcal{I}$ and $J \subseteq I$, then $J \in \mathcal{I}$.
  • (I3) Exchange. If $I, J \in \mathcal{I}$ with $|I| < |J|$, there exists $e \in J \setminus I$ such that $I \cup \{e\} \in \mathcal{I}$.

(I1) and (I2) alone define a simplicial complex — a downward-closed family. The novel content is (I3), the augmentation or exchange axiom: any smaller independent set can always be enlarged from any strictly larger one. It forces every maximal independent set to have the same size; without it, "independent" becomes a meaningless label.

Two motivating examples

Both examples below satisfy (I1)–(I3); the abstract definition is engineered to capture the common skeleton.

  • Vector matroid $M[A]$. Let $A$ be an $r \times n$ matrix over a field $k$, with columns indexed by $E = \{1,\ldots,n\}$. Set $\mathcal{I} = \{S \subseteq E : \text{columns}\ \{a_i : i \in S\}\ \text{are linearly independent}\}$. Hereditary is obvious; exchange is the Steinitz exchange lemma from linear algebra.
  • Graphic matroid $M(G)$. Let $G$ be a finite graph with edge set $E$. Set $\mathcal{I} = \{F \subseteq E : F\ \text{contains no cycle}\}$ — the forests of $G$. Hereditary because removing edges cannot create a cycle. Exchange: if $F, F'$ are forests with $|F| < |F'|$, then $F$ has more connected components than $F'$, so some edge of $F'$ joins two components of $F$ and can be added without forming a cycle.
Why the same axioms? The Steinitz exchange and the forest exchange feel like different facts. The matroid abstraction says they are not — both are instances of (I3). The dictionary then propagates: a basis (max independent), a circuit (min dependent), a dual matroid, a rank function, etc., all have parallel meanings on both sides. The pages on field extensions and cycles in algebraic topology are downstream consumers of this skeleton.

<script>
/* ============================ widget 1: axiom checker ============================ */
(function(){
  const Ein = $('#axioms-E'), Iin = $('#axioms-I'), out = $('#axioms-out');
  const btnCheck = $('#axioms-check'), btnUni = $('#axioms-uniform'), btnBad = $('#axioms-bad');

  function parseE(s){
    return s.split(',').map(t=>t.trim()).filter(Boolean);
  }
  function parseI(s){
    const lines = s.split('\n').map(t=>t.trim()).filter(Boolean);
    const out = [];
    for(const ln of lines){
      const m = ln.match(/^\{(.*)\}$/);
      if(!m) return null;
      const inner = m[1].trim();
      const els = inner ? inner.split(',').map(t=>t.trim()).filter(Boolean) : [];
      out.push(new Set(els));
    }
    return out;
  }
  function setEq(a,b){ if(a.size!==b.size) return false; for(const x of a) if(!b.has(x)) return false; return true; }
  function setIn(s, list){ return list.some(t=>setEq(s,t)); }
  function subsetOf(a,b){ for(const x of a) if(!b.has(x)) return false; return true; }

  function runCheck(){
    const E = parseE(Ein.value);
    const I = parseI(Iin.value);
    if(!I){ out.textContent = 'parse error: each line must look like {a,b,c} or {}'; return; }
    const lines = [];
    // (I1)
    const hasEmpty = I.some(s=>s.size===0);
    lines.push((hasEmpty ? '✓' : '✗') + ' (I1) non-trivial: ' + (hasEmpty ? '∅ is independent' : '∅ is missing — fail'));
    // (I2) hereditary
    let heredOk = true; let heredWit = null;
    for(const J of I){
      // every subset of J should be in I
      const els = [...J];
      const n = els.length;
      for(let mask=0; mask<(1<<n); mask++){
        const sub = new Set();
        for(let k=0;k<n;k++) if(mask&(1<<k)) sub.add(els[k]);
        if(!setIn(sub,I)){ heredOk=false; heredWit = {sub, sup:J}; break; }
      }
      if(!heredOk) break;
    }
    if(heredOk){
      lines.push('✓ (I2) hereditary: every subset of an independent set is independent');
    } else {
      const fmt = s => '{' + [...s].join(',') + '}';
      lines.push('✗ (I2) hereditary fails: ' + fmt(heredWit.sub) + ' ⊂ ' + fmt(heredWit.sup) + ' but only the larger is listed');
    }
    // (I3) exchange
    let exOk = true; let exWit = null;
    for(const A of I) for(const B of I){
      if(A.size >= B.size) continue;
      // need some e in B\A with A∪{e} ∈ I
      let found = false;
      for(const e of B){
        if(A.has(e)) continue;
        const cand = new Set([...A, e]);
        if(setIn(cand, I)){ found = true; break; }
      }
      if(!found){ exOk = false; exWit = {A,B}; break; }
    }
    if(exOk){
      lines.push('✓ (I3) exchange: any smaller independent set can be augmented from a larger one');
    } else {
      const fmt = s => '{' + [...s].join(',') + '}';
      lines.push('✗ (I3) exchange fails: from A=' + fmt(exWit.A) + ', no element of B=' + fmt(exWit.B) + '\\A produces a listed independent set');
    }
    const allOk = hasEmpty && heredOk && exOk;
    lines.push('');
    lines.push(allOk
      ? 'verdict: this family IS a matroid on ground set {' + E.join(',') + '}.'
      : 'verdict: NOT a matroid — fix the failing axiom above.');
    out.textContent = lines.join('\n');
  }
  btnCheck.addEventListener('click', runCheck);
  btnUni.addEventListener('click', ()=>{
    Ein.value = 'a,b,c,d';
    Iin.value = '{}\n{a}\n{b}\n{c}\n{d}\n{a,b}\n{a,c}\n{a,d}\n{b,c}\n{b,d}\n{c,d}';
    runCheck();
  });
  btnBad.addEventListener('click', ()=>{
    // hereditary holds, but exchange fails: bases of different sizes
    Ein.value = 'a,b,c,d';
    Iin.value = '{}\n{a}\n{b}\n{c}\n{d}\n{a,b}\n{a,b,c}';
    runCheck();
  });
  runCheck();
})();
</script>
</section>

2. Bases, rank, and cryptomorphism

A maximal independent set is a basis. The exchange axiom (I3) forces all bases to have the same cardinality, called the rank $r(M)$. More generally, for any $S \subseteq E$ define $$r(S) \;=\; \max\{\,|I| : I \subseteq S,\ I \in \mathcal{I}\,\}.$$ This rank function $r : 2^E \to \mathbb{Z}_{\ge 0}$ is the second face of a matroid.

Basis exchange

From (I3) one extracts the cleaner symmetric statement: for any two bases $B, B'$ and any element $x \in B \setminus B'$, there exists $y \in B' \setminus B$ with $(B \setminus \{x\}) \cup \{y\}$ also a basis. You "swap one in, one out." This basis-exchange axiom is itself enough to recover the whole matroid — the family of bases determines $\mathcal{I}$ as $\{I : I \subseteq B\ \text{for some basis}\ B\}$.

Rank-function axioms

The rank function is characterized by three properties:

  • (R1) $0 \le r(S) \le |S|$.
  • (R2) Monotone: $S \subseteq T \implies r(S) \le r(T)$.
  • (R3) Submodular: $r(S \cup T) + r(S \cap T) \le r(S) + r(T)$.

Conversely, any function $r$ satisfying (R1)–(R3) with integer values defines a matroid by $\mathcal{I} = \{S : r(S) = |S|\}$ — these are the sets where every element contributes one to the rank. The phenomenon that bases, independent sets, rank, closure, and circuits each give an equivalent axiomatization of the same object is called cryptomorphism; one matroid wears many costumes.

Submodularity is the heart. The inequality $r(S \cup T) + r(S \cap T) \le r(S) + r(T)$ is the combinatorial fingerprint that distinguishes matroids from arbitrary simplicial complexes. It says rank exhibits "diminishing returns": adding $T$ on top of $S$ gains less than adding $T$ to a smaller intersection. This is exactly why the greedy algorithm works (§ 6).

      t.textContent = String(i+1);
      svg.appendChild(t);
    }
    for(let j=0;j<bases.length;j++){

      for(let i=0;i<n;i++){

    // legend swatches at bottom

    const swatches = [

      ['var(--cyan)',   'in basis ∩ S'],
      ['var(--violet)', 'in S, not in basis'],
      ['var(--panel2)', 'in neither']
    ];
    let lx = pad;
    for(const [c, lbl] of swatches){

      const t = SVG('text',{x:lx+14, y:legY+9, 'font-size':11, fill:'var(--ink)'});
      t.textContent = lbl;
      svg.appendChild(t);
      lx += 8 + 14 + 8 + (lbl.length*6);

3. Vector, graphic, uniform, transversal

The class of matroids is wide enough to be interesting and narrow enough to be tractable. Four canonical families:

Uniform matroid $U_{r,n}$

Ground set $\{1,\ldots,n\}$; independent sets are all subsets of size $\le r$. Every $r$-subset is a basis; the rank function is $r(S) = \min(|S|, r)$. The case $U_{n,n}$ is "everything is independent" (the free matroid); $U_{0,n}$ is the trivial matroid. $U_{2,4}$ is the smallest non-representable example over $\mathbb{F}_2$ — it cannot arise as a vector matroid over the two-element field — and is a useful sanity-check counterexample.

Vector matroid $M[A]$

Already seen. A matroid is $k$-representable if it equals $M[A]$ for some matrix $A$ over the field $k$. Not every matroid is representable — the smallest non-representable example is the Vámos matroid on $8$ elements, which fails to be a vector matroid over any field. Tutte (1958) gave an excluded-minor characterization of $\mathbb{F}_2$-representable matroids: $M$ is binary $\iff$ $U_{2,4}$ is not a minor of $M$.

Graphic matroid $M(G)$

Independent sets = forests of $G$. Bases = spanning forests; if $G$ is connected, bases = spanning trees. The rank is $r(M(G)) = |V(G)| - c(G)$ where $c$ counts connected components. Graphic matroids are always $\mathbb{F}_2$-representable (and in fact $\mathbb{F}_p$-representable for every $p$): take the signed incidence matrix.

Transversal matroid

Given a finite collection $\mathcal{A} = (A_1, \ldots, A_m)$ of subsets of $E$, declare $S \subseteq E$ independent if there is an injective system of distinct representatives — an injection $\phi : S \to \{1,\ldots,m\}$ with $s \in A_{\phi(s)}$ for all $s \in S$. Hall's marriage theorem shows this is a matroid; bases are maximum partial transversals. Transversal matroids are exactly the matroids of certain bipartite-graph matchings, and form the bridge between matroid theory and combinatorial optimization.

<script>
/* ============================ widget 3: graphic matroid (K4) ============================ */
(function(){
  const svg = $('#graph-svg'), out = $('#graph-out');
  const btnClear = $('#graph-clear'), btnSpan = $('#graph-spanning');

  // K_4: vertices at corners of a square, all pairs connected.
  const V = [
    {id:0, x:160, y: 60},
    {id:1, x:460, y: 60},
    {id:2, x:160, y:260},
    {id:3, x:460, y:260}
  ];
  const Eraw = [];
  for(let i=0;i<V.length;i++) for(let j=i+1;j<V.length;j++) Eraw.push({u:i, v:j, id:`${i}-${j}`});

  let F = new Set(); // selected edge ids

  // union–find on V
  function uf(){
    const p = V.map((_,i)=>i);
    function find(x){ while(p[x]!==x){ p[x]=p[p[x]]; x=p[x]; } return x; }
    function union(x,y){ const rx=find(x), ry=find(y); if(rx!==ry){ p[rx]=ry; return true; } return false; }
    return {find, union, p};
  }

  function analyze(){
    const u = uf();
    const safe = new Set(), cyc = new Set();
    for(const e of Eraw){
      if(!F.has(e.id)) continue;
      if(u.union(e.u, e.v)) safe.add(e.id);
      else cyc.add(e.id);
    }
    const comps = new Set();
    for(let i=0;i<V.length;i++) comps.add(u.find(i));
    const rank = V.length - comps.size;
    return {safe, cyc, rank, comps:comps.size};
  }

  function render(){
    svg.innerHTML = '';
    const {safe, cyc, rank, comps} = analyze();
    // edges first
    for(const e of Eraw){
      const a = V[e.u], b = V[e.v];
      let stroke = 'var(--line)', width = 2, opacity = 0.7;
      if(safe.has(e.id)){ stroke = 'var(--yellow)'; width = 4; opacity = 1; }
      else if(cyc.has(e.id)){ stroke = 'var(--pink)'; width = 4; opacity = 1; }
      const path = SVG('line',{x1:a.x, y1:a.y, x2:b.x, y2:b.y, stroke, 'stroke-width':width, opacity, 'data-edge':e.id, 'pointer-events':'stroke'});
      path.style.cursor = 'pointer';
      path.addEventListener('click', ()=>{
        if(F.has(e.id)) F.delete(e.id); else F.add(e.id);
        render();
      });
      // hit-area
      const hit = SVG('line',{x1:a.x, y1:a.y, x2:b.x, y2:b.y, stroke:'transparent','stroke-width':16, 'data-edge':e.id});
      hit.style.cursor='pointer';
      hit.addEventListener('click', ()=>{
        if(F.has(e.id)) F.delete(e.id); else F.add(e.id);
        render();
      });
      svg.appendChild(hit);
      svg.appendChild(path);
      // edge label at midpoint
      const mx = (a.x+b.x)/2, my = (a.y+b.y)/2;
      const lbl = SVG('text',{x:mx + (e.u===0&&e.v===3?-8:0), y:my - 6, 'text-anchor':'middle','font-size':12, fill:'var(--mute)'});
      lbl.textContent = `e_{${e.u},${e.v}}`;
      svg.appendChild(lbl);
    }
    // vertices
    for(const v of V){
      svg.appendChild(SVG('circle',{cx:v.x, cy:v.y, r:18, fill:'var(--panel2)', stroke:'var(--blue)', 'stroke-width':1.6}));
      const t = SVG('text',{x:v.x, y:v.y+5, 'text-anchor':'middle', 'font-size':14, 'font-weight':600});
      t.textContent = String(v.id+1);
      svg.appendChild(t);
    }
    const total = F.size;
    const lines = [];
    lines.push(`|F| = ${total}`);
    lines.push(`safe edges (forest part): ${safe.size}`);
    lines.push(`cycle-creating edges:    ${cyc.size}    ${cyc.size>0?'⇒ F is dependent':'⇒ F is independent (a forest)'}`);
    lines.push(`rank r(F) = ${rank}    (= |V| − components = 4 − ${comps})`);
    if(rank===3 && cyc.size===0) lines.push('F is a basis (spanning tree).');
    out.textContent = lines.join('\n');
  }
  btnClear.addEventListener('click', ()=>{ F.clear(); render(); });
  btnSpan.addEventListener('click', ()=>{
    F = new Set(['0-1','0-2','0-3']); // star at vertex 0 spans
    render();
  });
  render();
})();
</script>
</section>

4. Closure, flats, and the geometric lattice

For $S \subseteq E$, the closure $\operatorname{cl}(S)$ is $$\operatorname{cl}(S) \;=\; \{\,e \in E : r(S \cup \{e\}) = r(S)\,\}.$$ That is, $\operatorname{cl}(S)$ contains every element that contributes nothing extra to the rank — exactly the "linear span" intuition for the vector-matroid case. A subset $F \subseteq E$ is a flat (or closed set) if $F = \operatorname{cl}(F)$.

Closure axioms

The closure operator $\operatorname{cl}: 2^E \to 2^E$ satisfies:

  • (C1) Extensive: $S \subseteq \operatorname{cl}(S)$.
  • (C2) Monotone: $S \subseteq T \implies \operatorname{cl}(S) \subseteq \operatorname{cl}(T)$.
  • (C3) Idempotent: $\operatorname{cl}(\operatorname{cl}(S)) = \operatorname{cl}(S)$.
  • (C4) Mac Lane–Steinitz exchange: if $y \in \operatorname{cl}(S \cup \{x\}) \setminus \operatorname{cl}(S)$, then $x \in \operatorname{cl}(S \cup \{y\})$.

(C1)–(C3) make $\operatorname{cl}$ a closure operator on a poset; the matroid content is (C4), which says "if $y$ depended on $S \cup \{x\}$ but not on $S$, then $x$ depended on $S \cup \{y\}$" — the closure-side avatar of the exchange axiom. Any operator satisfying (C1)–(C4) on $2^E$ is the closure of a unique matroid.

The lattice of flats

Order flats by inclusion. The result is a geometric lattice: graded (by rank), atomistic (every flat is the join of rank-$1$ flats, which are the singletons or "points"), and semimodular ($r(F \vee G) + r(F \wedge G) \le r(F) + r(G)$). Birkhoff's theorem (1935) says geometric lattices and simple matroids — those without loops or parallel elements — are the same data.

Projective planes are matroids. Take the points of $\mathrm{PG}(2,q)$ as the ground set; declare a $3$-subset independent unless its three points are collinear. The result is a rank-$3$ matroid whose flats are: $\emptyset$, the points, the lines, and $E$. The geometric-lattice structure recovers the incidence axioms of the projective plane. See projective plane § axioms.

Flats of $M(K_4)$

In the graphic matroid $M(G)$, flats correspond to edge sets closed under "complete-the-cycle." Rank-$0$ flat: $\emptyset$. Rank-$1$ flats: each single edge (a "point"). Rank-$2$ flats: the "triangles" — the three edges of any $K_3$ subgraph — plus the disjoint pairs of edges, all closed. Rank-$3$ flat: $E$ itself.

5. Matroid duality

Every matroid $M$ on ground set $E$ has a dual matroid $M^*$ on the same ground set, defined by $$\mathcal{B}(M^*) \;=\; \{\, E \setminus B : B \in \mathcal{B}(M)\,\}.$$ The bases of $M^*$ are precisely the complements of the bases of $M$. Verifying this satisfies the basis-exchange axiom is a one-line consequence of the original exchange — and the construction is involutive: $(M^*)^* = M$.

Dual rank

The rank functions are linked by $$r_{M^*}(S) \;=\; |S| - r(M) + r(E \setminus S).$$ In particular $r(M) + r(M^*) = |E|$. Loops of $M$ are coloops of $M^*$; circuits of $M^*$ are cocircuits of $M$, the minimal cuts that disconnect a basis.

The dual of a graphic matroid

Here is the punchline. If $G$ is a planar graph and $G^*$ is its planar dual (vertex per face, edge per shared boundary), then $$M(G)^* \;=\; M(G^*).$$ Spanning trees of $G$ are in bijection with spanning trees of $G^*$ via edge-complement. For non-planar $G$, $M(G)^*$ exists as a matroid but is no longer graphic — Whitney's planarity criterion (1932) says $G$ is planar iff $M(G)^*$ is graphic, with the dual graph providing a witness. The same matroid-theoretic statement subsumes Kuratowski's $K_5$/$K_{3,3}$ criterion.

Every theorem has a co-theorem. A statement about matroids automatically has a dual: replace "basis" by "complement of basis", "circuit" by "cocircuit", "rank" by the dual formula. The greedy algorithm § 6 has a dual interpretation as a "reverse-greedy" deletion procedure. Tutte polynomial § 7 is symmetric under $T_M(x,y) = T_{M^*}(y,x)$. This duality is the matroid-theoretic shadow of the planar-graph face/vertex duality and of the linear-algebra duality between row space and column space.

6. The greedy algorithm

Suppose each $e \in E$ has a non-negative weight $w(e)$, and we want a maximum-weight independent set. The greedy algorithm:

  1. Sort the elements so that $w(e_1) \ge w(e_2) \ge \cdots \ge w(e_n)$.
  2. Initialize $I \leftarrow \emptyset$.
  3. For $i = 1, 2, \ldots, n$: if $I \cup \{e_i\} \in \mathcal{I}$, set $I \leftarrow I \cup \{e_i\}$.

Output $I$. The cost is $O(n \log n)$ for the sort plus $n$ independence tests. The classic application is Kruskal's minimum-spanning-tree algorithm: take the graphic matroid, weights = edge weights, greedy = Kruskal.

Rado–Edmonds: matroids are exactly where greedy works

The deepest theorem of elementary matroid theory:

Theorem (Rado 1957, Edmonds 1971). Let $\mathcal{I}$ be a non-empty hereditary family on $E$. The greedy algorithm finds a maximum-weight independent set for every non-negative weight function $w$ if and only if $\mathcal{I}$ satisfies the exchange axiom (I3) — i.e. iff $(E, \mathcal{I})$ is a matroid.

One direction ($\Leftarrow$): suppose $\mathcal{I}$ is a matroid and the greedy returns $I = \{e_{i_1}, \ldots, e_{i_k}\}$ with $w(e_{i_1}) \ge \cdots \ge w(e_{i_k})$. Suppose for contradiction some $J \in \mathcal{I}$ has strictly greater total weight, with $J = \{e_{j_1}, \ldots, e_{j_\ell}\}$ in decreasing order. Both $I$ and $J$ are independent and $|I|, |J| \le r(M)$. Apply (I3) to extend $I_{ w(I)$.

The reverse direction ($\Rightarrow$) is more striking: hereditary plus "greedy is always optimal" forces the exchange axiom. So matroids are characterized by the optimality of greedy. Whenever a combinatorial-optimization problem on a hereditary family is solved by sort-and-take, the underlying structure is a matroid; whenever the greedy fails on some instance, you have built a non-matroid.

Matroid intersection (sketch)

Given two matroids $M_1, M_2$ on the same ground set, finding a maximum-cardinality common independent set $I \in \mathcal{I}_1 \cap \mathcal{I}_2$ is solvable in polynomial time (Edmonds 1970). The intersection captures bipartite matching ($M_1, M_2$ = partition matroids on the two sides), arborescences (graphic + partition), and many more. Three matroids' intersection is NP-hard; two is the boundary of tractability.

7. The Tutte polynomial

The deepest invariant of a matroid is a two-variable polynomial whose specializations enumerate spanning trees, count colorings, compute reliability, and read out the partition function of statistical-mechanics models. Define the Tutte polynomial via corank–nullity: $$T_M(x,y) \;=\; \sum_{S \subseteq E} (x-1)^{r(E) - r(S)} \, (y-1)^{|S| - r(S)}.$$ The exponent $r(E) - r(S)$ is the corank of $S$ (how far short of full rank); $|S| - r(S)$ is the nullity (how many cycles $S$ contains). Despite the strange shifts, $T_M(x,y) \in \mathbb{Z}_{\ge 0}[x,y]$ — every coefficient is a non-negative integer (Tutte 1947).

The deletion–contraction recursion

For an element $e \in E$ that is neither a loop nor a coloop: $$T_M(x,y) \;=\; T_{M \setminus e}(x,y) + T_{M / e}(x,y).$$ Here $M \setminus e$ is the matroid deletion (remove $e$, restrict $\mathcal{I}$) and $M/e$ is the contraction (treat $e$ as committed; rank drops by $1$). Loops and coloops produce boundary cases: $T_M = y \cdot T_{M \setminus e}$ if $e$ is a loop, and $T_M = x \cdot T_{M / e}$ if $e$ is a coloop. The recursion plus the boundary identities computes $T_M$ from scratch in polynomial-time-per-leaf but exponentially-many-leaves recursion: deciding whether $T_M(x,y) = 0$ at a generic point is $\#P$-hard.

Specializations

evaluationmeaning
$T_M(1,1)$number of bases of $M$
$T_M(2,1)$number of independent sets
$T_M(1,2)$number of spanning sets (sets containing a basis)
$T_M(2,2)$$2^{|E|}$ — every subset, weighted appropriately
$k^{c(G)} (-1)^{r(M)} T_{M(G)}(1-k,0)$chromatic polynomial $P_G(k)$ of the graph $G$ ($c(G) =$ components)
$(-1)^{|E|-r(M)} T_{M(G)}(0, 1-k)$flow polynomial of $G$
$T_{M(G)}(1, 1/(1-p))$$($up to a factor$)$ all-terminal reliability of $G$ at edge-failure $p$
$T_M(x,y)$partition function of the random-cluster Potts model

Each row in this table is a separate combinatorial-or-physical theory. The Tutte polynomial unifies them: pick the right substitution and the answer falls out. The duality $T_M(x,y) = T_{M^*}(y,x)$ exchanges chromatic and flow specializations — explaining, on the matroid side, why coloring and flow are dual stories on planar graphs.

Whitney rank polynomial. Some authors use $R_M(u,v) = \sum_{S \subseteq E} u^{r(E)-r(S)} v^{|S|-r(S)} = T_M(u+1, v+1)$. The Tutte normalization shifts the variables so that $T_M(1,1)$ counts bases. Same polynomial; choose your conventions.

Tutte polynomial calculator
pick a small matroid; compute $T_M(x,y)$ and selected evaluations
1.0 1.0
$T_M(x,y)$ is computed directly from $\sum_S (x-1)^{r(E)-r(S)}(y-1)^{|S|-r(S)}$ over all $2^{|E|}$ subsets. The slider evaluates at user-specified $(x,y)$; the table beneath lists the canonical evaluations.

<script>
/* ============================ widget 7: Tutte polynomial ============================ */
(function(){
  const out = $('#tutte-out');
  const xIn = $('#tutte-x'), yIn = $('#tutte-y');
  const xv = $('#tutte-xv'), yv = $('#tutte-yv');
  const btnU = $('#tutte-uni'), btnK3 = $('#tutte-k3'), btnK4 = $('#tutte-k4');

  // matroid descriptors: provide rank function on subsets given as bitmask of |E| elements.
  const MS = {
    'U_{2,4}': {
      n: 4,
      rank: F => Math.min(F.length, 2),
      desc: 'uniform U_{2,4}: r(S) = min(|S|, 2),  r(E)=2,  |E|=4'
    },
    'M(K_3)': {
      n: 3, // triangle on 3 vertices, 3 edges; rank 2
      // edges e_1=12, e_2=13, e_3=23
      Eedges:[[0,1],[0,2],[1,2]],
      rank: function(F){
        const p=[0,1,2];
        function find(x){ while(p[x]!==x){ p[x]=p[p[x]]; x=p[x]; } return x; }
        for(const i of F){ const [u,v]=this.Eedges[i]; const ru=find(u),rv=find(v); if(ru!==rv) p[ru]=rv; }
        const c=new Set(); for(let i=0;i<3;i++) c.add(find(i));
        return 3-c.size;
      },
      desc: 'graphic M(K_3): triangle.  |E|=3, r(E)=2.'
    },
    'M(K_4)': {
      n: 6,
      Eedges:[[0,1],[0,2],[0,3],[1,2],[1,3],[2,3]],
      rank: function(F){
        const p=[0,1,2,3];
        function find(x){ while(p[x]!==x){ p[x]=p[p[x]]; x=p[x]; } return x; }
        for(const i of F){ const [u,v]=this.Eedges[i]; const ru=find(u),rv=find(v); if(ru!==rv) p[ru]=rv; }
        const c=new Set(); for(let i=0;i<4;i++) c.add(find(i));
        return 4-c.size;
      },
      desc: 'graphic M(K_4): complete graph.  |E|=6, r(E)=3.  16 spanning trees.'
    }
  };

  function tutteCoeffs(M){
    // returns array coeffs[i][j] = coeff of (x-1)^i (y-1)^j (i.e. of u^i v^j after substitution),
    // grouped as flat coeffs in two-variable polynomial T(x,y) expressed in (x-1, y-1).
    const n = M.n, R = M.rank.bind(M);
    const coeffs = {};
    const rE = R(Array.from({length:n},(_,k)=>k));
    for(let mask=0; mask<(1<<n); mask++){
      const F=[]; for(let i=0;i<n;i++) if(mask&(1<<i)) F.push(i);
      const rF = R(F);
      const i = rE - rF, j = F.length - rF;
      const key = i+','+j;
      coeffs[key] = (coeffs[key]||0) + 1;
    }
    return {coeffs, rE, n};
  }
  function evalT(M, x, y){
    let s = 0;
    const n = M.n, R = M.rank.bind(M);
    const rE = R(Array.from({length:n},(_,k)=>k));
    for(let mask=0; mask<(1<<n); mask++){
      const F=[]; for(let i=0;i<n;i++) if(mask&(1<<i)) F.push(i);
      const rF = R(F);
      s += Math.pow(x-1, rE - rF) * Math.pow(y-1, F.length - rF);
    }
    return s;
  }
  function chrom(M, k){
    // P_G(k) = k^{c(G)} (-1)^{r} T(1-k, 0).  c(G) = #components.
    // For our connected graphs M(K_3), M(K_4): c = 1, but we compute mechanically.
    const T = evalT(M, 1-k, 0);
    const r = M.rank(Array.from({length:M.n},(_,i)=>i));
    // |V| = r + c, so c = |V| - r.  M(K_3): |V|=3, r=2 ⇒ c=1.  M(K_4): |V|=4, r=3 ⇒ c=1.
    const V = M.Eedges ? Math.max(...M.Eedges.flat()) + 1 : r + 1;
    const c = V - r;
    return Math.pow(k, c) * Math.pow(-1, r) * T;
  }

  function fmtPoly(coeffs){
    // express T(x,y) in terms of (x-1)^i (y-1)^j as sum
    const items = Object.entries(coeffs).map(([k,v])=>{
      const [i,j] = k.split(',').map(Number);
      const u = i===0 ? '' : (i===1 ? '(x−1)' : `(x−1)^${i}`);
      const w = j===0 ? '' : (j===1 ? '(y−1)' : `(y−1)^${j}`);
      const term = (u+w) || '1';
      return v + '·' + term;
    });
    return items.join(' + ');
  }

  let cur = 'U_{2,4}';
  function render(){
    btnU.classList.toggle('active', cur==='U_{2,4}');
    btnK3.classList.toggle('active', cur==='M(K_3)');
    btnK4.classList.toggle('active', cur==='M(K_4)');
    const M = MS[cur];
    const x = parseFloat(xIn.value), y = parseFloat(yIn.value);
    xv.textContent = x.toFixed(1);
    yv.textContent = y.toFixed(1);
    const {coeffs} = tutteCoeffs(M);

    const lines = [];
    lines.push(M.desc);
    lines.push('');
    lines.push('T_M(x,y) (in shifted variables (x−1, y−1) for compactness):');
    lines.push('  T = ' + fmtPoly(coeffs));
    lines.push('');
    lines.push(`evaluation at (${x.toFixed(1)}, ${y.toFixed(1)}):`);
    lines.push(`  T_M(${x.toFixed(1)}, ${y.toFixed(1)}) = ${evalT(M, x, y).toFixed(4)}`);
    lines.push('');
    lines.push('canonical evaluations:');
    lines.push(`  T(1,1) = ${Math.round(evalT(M,1,1))}    (number of bases)`);
    lines.push(`  T(2,1) = ${Math.round(evalT(M,2,1))}    (number of independent sets)`);
    lines.push(`  T(1,2) = ${Math.round(evalT(M,1,2))}    (number of spanning sets)`);
    lines.push(`  T(2,2) = ${Math.round(evalT(M,2,2))}    (= 2^{|E|} = ${Math.pow(2,M.n)})`);
    if(cur !== 'U_{2,4}'){
      lines.push('');
      lines.push('chromatic polynomial of the graph (k = number of colors):');
      for(const k of [2,3,4]){
        lines.push(`  P_G(${k}) = ${Math.round(chrom(M, k))}`);
      }
    }
    out.textContent = lines.join('\n');
  }

  btnU.addEventListener('click', ()=>{ cur='U_{2,4}'; render(); });
  btnK3.addEventListener('click', ()=>{ cur='M(K_3)'; render(); });
  btnK4.addEventListener('click', ()=>{ cur='M(K_4)'; render(); });
  xIn.addEventListener('input', render);
  yIn.addEventListener('input', render);
  render();
})();
</script>
</section>

8. Connections

Matroids axiomatise "linear independence" in a way that no longer requires linear algebra. Their bridges below are where the abstraction pays off.

Linear algebra and field-representability

Every finite-dimensional vector configuration is a matroid; the converse fails — there are matroids representable over no field at all. The classification of matroids representable over $\mathbb{F}_2, \mathbb{F}_3, \dots$ is one of the deep results of the subject.

Algebraic geometry and the Bergman fan

The Bergman fan of a matroid is the support of its Chow ring (Adiprasito–Huh–Katz). The hard Lefschetz theorem and Hodge–Riemann relations hold for these rings, providing the proof of Rota’s log-concavity conjecture.

Probabilistic and extremal combinatorics

The probabilistic method and extremal combinatorics use matroid-style independence arguments routinely; matroid-union and matroid-intersection theorems are deep tools for partition problems.

Optimisation and the greedy algorithm

The defining feature of a matroid is that the greedy algorithm computes the maximum-weight independent set. This is the right axiomatic setting for Edmonds-style polynomial-time combinatorial optimisation.

Open frontiers: tropical geometry of matroids (the Bergman fan / log-concavity link); the matroid axiomatisation of Coxeter groups (oriented matroids and arrangements); algebraic matroids and the field-of-definition problem.
